title: Featherstonhaugh
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Brookong station lay half-asleep
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dozed in the waning western glare
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (ן¿½Twas before the run had stocked with sheep
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And only cattle depastured there)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As the Bluccap mob reined up at the door
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And loudly saluted Featherstonhaugh.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½My saintly preacher,ן¿½ the leader cried,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I stand no nonsense, as youן¿½re aware,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½ve a word for you if youן¿½ll step outside,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Just drop that pistol and have a care;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½ll trouble you, too, for the key of the store,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For weן¿½re short of tucker, friend Featherstonhaugh.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The muscular Christian showed no fear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Though he handed the key with but small delay.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He never answered the ruffianן¿½s jeer
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Except by a look which seemed to say,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Beware, my friend, and think twice before
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You raise the devil in Featherstonhaugh.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Two hours after he reined his horse
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Up in Urana, and straightway went
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the barracks, the trooper was gone, of course,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Blindly nosing a week-old scent
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Away in the scrub around Mount Galore.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Confound the fellow!ן¿½ quoth Featherstonhaugh.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Will any man of you come with me
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And give this Bluecap a dressing-down?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They all regarded him silently
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As he turned his horse, with a scornful frown.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Youן¿½re curs, the lot of you, to the core,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Iן¿½ll go by myself,ן¿½ said Featherstonhaugh.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The scrub was thick on Urangeline
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As he followed the tracks that twisted through
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The box and dogwood and scented pine
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (One of their horses had cast a shoe).
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Steeped from his youth in forest lore,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He could track like a nigger, could Featherstonhaugh.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He paused as he saw the thread of smoke
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From the outlaw camp, and he marked the sound
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of a hobble-check, as it sharply broke
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The silence that held the scrub-land bound.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There were their horses, two, three, four,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Itן¿½s a risk, but Iן¿½ll chance it!ן¿½ quoth Featherstonhaugh.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He loosened the first, and it walked away,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But his comradeן¿½s silence could not be bought,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For he raised his head with a sudden neigh,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And plainly showed that heן¿½d not be caught.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As a bullet sang from a rifle-bore,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Itן¿½s time to be moving,ן¿½ quoth Featherstonhaugh.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The brittle pine, as they broke away,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Crackled like ice in a winterן¿½s ponds,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The strokes fell fast on the cones that lay
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Buried beneath the withered fronds
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That softly carpet the sandy floor,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Swept two on the tracks of Featherstonhaugh.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They struck that path that the stock had made,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A dustily-red, well-beaten track,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The leader opened a fusillade
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whose target was Featherstonן¿½s stooping back
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But his luck was out, not a bullet tore
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As much as a shred from Featherstonhaugh.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rattle ן¿½em, rattle ן¿½em fast on the pad,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where the sloping shades fell dusk and dim.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The managerן¿½s heart beat high and glad
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For he knew the creek was a mighty swim.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Already he heard a smothered roar,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Theyן¿½re done like a dinner!ן¿½ quoth Featherstonhaugh.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It was almost dark as they neared the dam.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He struck the crossing as true as a hair;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For the space of a second the pony swam,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then shook himself in the chill night air.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In a pine-tree shade on the further shore,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With his pistol cocked, stood Featherstonhaugh.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A splash, an oath, and a rearing horse,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A thread snapped short in the fateful loom,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The tide, unaltered, swept on its course
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Though a fellow creature had met his doom:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Pale and trembling, and struck with awe,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Bluccap stood opposite Featherstonhaugh.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While the creek rolled muddily in between
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The eddies played with the drowned manן¿½s hat.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The stars peeped out in the summer sheen,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A night-bird chirruped across the flat,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Quoth Bluecap, ן¿½I owe you a heavy score,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And Iן¿½ll live to repay it, Featherstonhaugh.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But he never did, for he ran his race
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Before he had time to fulfil his oath.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I canן¿½t think how, but, in any case,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He was hung, or drowned, or maybe both.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But whichever it was, he came no more
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To trouble the peace of Featherstonhaugh.
meaning:
About: Barcroft Henry Thomas Boake (March 26, 1866 ן¿½ found dead May 10, 1892) was an Australian poet.

Born in Sydney, Boake worked as a surveyor and a boundary rider in New South Wales and Queensland, but is best remembered for his poetry, a volume of which was published five years after his death. A sufferer of bipolar disorder, he is believed to have committed suicide. His body was found hanging by the neck eight days after he disappeared on May 2, 1892, from a stockwhip at Middle Harbour in Sydney. One writer on Boake's life has mentioned that the suicide took place during the 1891-93 depression when the poet was unable to find work, also noting that "it has been suggested that he killed himself for the love of one of the McKeahnie girls," sisters of the horseman Charlie McKeahnie.
